children.name: Batagur
children.description: Large river turtles of South and Southeast Asia, including the former genera Callagur and Kachuga. These are among the most endangered turtles on Earth, with all six species listed as Critically Endangered or Endangered. They inhabit major river systems and estuaries, where they nest on sandy riverbanks. Males of several species develop striking breeding coloration — white heads with vivid red or orange crowns.

children.name: Batagur affinis
children.description: A large river turtle reaching 50 cm in carapace length, with a brownish-grey carapace and a yellowish plastron. Males develop a bright white head with a red crown during the breeding season. Found in Cambodia, Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam, inhabiting large rivers and estuaries; critically endangered from overharvesting and habitat loss.

children.name: Batagur baska
children.description: A massive freshwater turtle reaching 60 cm and 25 kg, with a dark brown carapace and webbed feet adapted for powerful swimming. Historically ranged from Bangladesh and India through Myanmar to Malaysia, now extirpated from most of its range; survives in a few fragmented populations. Nests on sandy riverbanks; critically endangered from egg collection and habitat modification.

children.name: Batagur borneoensis
children.description: Named for the vivid white head with a bright red stripe that breeding males display; females are larger but duller. Inhabits brackish estuaries, mangrove forests, and tidal rivers of Indonesia, Malaysia, and Thailand. Nests on coastal beaches; critically endangered due to egg poaching, bycatch, and coastal development.

children.name: Batagur dhongoka
children.description: A medium-large turtle with a prominently keeled carapace bearing three distinct ridges, giving it a roofed appearance. Found in the Ganges and Brahmaputra river systems of India, Nepal, and Bangladesh. Inhabits deep river channels and nests on sandy banks; critically endangered from sand mining, fishing nets, and egg collection.

children.name: Batagur kachuga
children.description: One of the most threatened turtles in the world, with a strikingly keeled carapace and a red-crowned head in breeding males. Historically widespread in the Ganges and Brahmaputra basins, now reduced to a single viable population in the National Chambal Sanctuary, India. Critically endangered; fewer than 500 adults remain in the wild.

children.name: Batagur trivittata
children.description: A large river turtle with three prominent keels on the carapace and a distinctive olive-green coloration. Endemic to Myanmar, restricted to the upper Chindwin River; was thought extinct until rediscovered in 2002. Extremely rare, with a small captive breeding population; critically endangered from habitat alteration and traditional use.

children.name: Cuora
children.description: Asian box turtles — freshwater turtles with a hinged plastron that allows the shell to close almost completely, enclosing the head and limbs. This defensive adaptation is convergent with the American box turtles of genus Terrapene, to which they are unrelated. Most species are critically endangered due to mass collection for traditional medicine and the food trade — some are among the most threatened reptiles on Earth.

children.name: Cuora aurocapitata
children.description: A small, brightly coloured box turtle with a striking yellow or orange head and a dark brown carapace marked with a pale vertebral stripe. Endemic to Anhui Province, China, where it inhabits slow-moving streams and ponds in hilly regions. Critically endangered from overcollection for traditional medicine and the pet trade.

children.name: Cuora bourreti
children.description: A recently elevated species from the Cuora galbinifrons complex, with a brown carapace and a yellow-orange head marked by a dark postorbital stripe. Inhabits evergreen forests of central Vietnam and adjacent Laos. Critically endangered from habitat loss and intense collection for food and traditional medicine.

children.name: Cuora cyclornata
children.description: A large Cuora species closely related to the Golden Coin Turtle, with three distinct black stripes on a pale brown carapace and a yellowish head and neck. Found in northern and central Vietnam and adjacent southern China. Critically endangered; heavily exploited for traditional medicine and the luxury food market, where large specimens command extremely high prices.

children.name: Cuora galbinifrons
children.description: A variable species with a domed, often patterned carapace ranging from brown to yellow and a pale yellow head with dark markings. Occurs in evergreen and karst forests of southern China, Vietnam, Laos, and northeastern Cambodia. Critically endangered from massive overcollection for Chinese traditional medicine and the food trade.

children.name: Cuora mccordi
children.description: A small, brightly coloured box turtle with a pale yellow to orange head, a brown carapace with a pale central stripe, and a fully hinged plastron. Known only from a small region in Guangxi, China, possibly extinct in the wild; persists in captivity. Critically endangered due to extreme overcollection for the pet and medicine trades.

children.name: Cuora mouhotii
children.description: Distinguished by its strongly keeled, elongated carapace with serrated marginal scutes, giving it a saw-backed appearance. Inhabits hill and montane evergreen forests of northeastern India, Myanmar, Thailand, Laos, Vietnam, and southern China. Endangered from habitat loss and collection for the pet trade and traditional medicine.

children.name: Cuora pani
children.description: A medium-sized box turtle with a brown carapace, pale head stripes, and a fully hinged plastron enabling complete shell closure. Known only from a few localities in Shaanxi and Hubei provinces, China, in hill streams and nearby pools. Critically endangered; extremely rare in the wild due to overcollection.

children.name: Cuora picturata
children.description: A colourful member of the C. galbinifrons complex with a yellow-tan carapace overlain with bold dark blotches and a bright yellow head. Confined to the Langbian Plateau of southern Vietnam in montane evergreen forest. Critically endangered from deforestation and collection for the pet trade.

children.name: Cuora yunnanensis
children.description: A medium-sized box turtle with a brown carapace bearing a pale central stripe and a yellow head with dark-edged markings. Endemic to Yunnan Province, China, where it inhabits mountain streams and ponds. Considered possibly extinct in the wild for decades until rediscovered in the pet trade; critically endangered from overcollection.

children.name: Cuora zhoui
children.description: A recently described species with an olive-brown carapace, a pale yellow head, and a characteristic dark blotch behind the eye. Known only from a small area in Guangxi and Yunnan, China, and possibly northern Vietnam. Critically endangered; virtually unknown in the wild and known mostly from specimens in the Asian food markets.

children.name: Cyclemys
children.description: Leaf turtles of South and Southeast Asia — medium-sized freshwater turtles with a flattened, oval carapace that resembles a dead leaf, providing camouflage on the forest floor. They inhabit hill streams, mountain brooks, and shaded pools in evergreen and deciduous forests. The taxonomy of the genus has been revised significantly in recent years, with several former subspecies elevated to full species.

children.name: Cyclemys atripons
children.description: A medium-sized leaf turtle with a brown carapace and a distinct black bridge connecting the plastron to the carapace. Found in southeastern Thailand and Cambodia, inhabiting slow-moving forest streams and pools. Often confused with C. oldhamii but distinguished by genetic and morphological characters.

children.name: Cyclemys dentata
children.description: The most widespread Cyclemys species, with a flattened brown carapace that resembles a fallen leaf and serrated marginal scutes. Ranges from northeastern India and Bangladesh through Myanmar, Thailand, Laos, Cambodia, Vietnam, Malaysia, Indonesia, and the Philippines. Inhabits slow-moving forest streams; threatened by habitat loss and collection.

children.name: Cyclemys enigmatica
children.description: A recently described species with a dark brown carapace, a broad dark bridge, and a uniformly coloured head without markings. Found on Borneo (Sarawak, Kalimantan) and possibly Sumatra. Little is known of its ecology; inhabits lowland forest streams.

children.name: Cyclemys fusca
children.description: A dark-coloured leaf turtle with a uniformly brown carapace, a dark plastron, and an unmarked brown head. Endemic to Myanmar, where it inhabits hill streams and pools in evergreen forests. Previously confused with C. oldhamii but distinguished by its darker colouration and genetic divergence.

children.name: Cyclemys oldhamii
children.description: A medium-sized leaf turtle with a brown carapace that may bear darker radiating lines on each scute, and a pale brownish head. Ranges from Myanmar and Thailand through Indochina to Sumatra and Java. Inhabits lowland and hill forest streams; threatened by habitat conversion and collection for the pet trade.

children.name: Cyclemys pulchristriata
children.description: A distinctive species with fine yellow or light brown stripes radiating across each scute of the carapace, giving a beautifully patterned appearance. Found in northeastern India (Assam, Arunachal Pradesh) and adjacent Bangladesh. Inhabits hill streams and temporary forest pools.

children.name: Emys
children.description: A relict genus of freshwater turtles that once ranged across most of Europe and now survives as an increasingly fragmented population in southern and central Europe. The European Pond Turtle is the only native freshwater turtle in much of continental Europe — a reminder that reptiles were far more diverse here before Pleistocene glaciations repeatedly compressed their range southward. It basks conspicuously on logs and banks, but slips into the water with remarkable speed when alarmed.

children.name: Geoclemys
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the black pond turtle of South Asia. It is distinguished by its heavily spotted head and limbs — a pattern of bright yellow spots on a black background that is unique among geoemydids. Once considered part of the genus Damonia, molecular studies place it as a distinct lineage within the Geoemydinae.

children.name: Geoclemys hamiltonii
children.description: A striking freshwater turtle with a black or dark brown carapace and a dense pattern of bright yellow spots covering the head, neck, and limbs. Found in the Indus, Ganges, and Brahmaputra river systems of Pakistan, India, Bangladesh, and Nepal. Inhabits slow-moving rivers, ponds, and wetlands; endangered from water pollution, habitat loss, and collection for the pet trade.

children.name: Geoemyda
children.description: Leaf turtles of East and Southeast Asia — small, semi-terrestrial turtles with a flattened, serrated carapace that mimics dead leaves for camouflage. They inhabit mountain forests and hill streams from southern China and Japan to Vietnam. Both species are threatened by habitat loss and overcollection for the international pet trade.

children.name: Geoemyda japonica
children.description: Endemic to the Ryukyu Islands of Japan, with a flattened brown carapace and serrated posterior marginals. The plastron is dark brown to black. Inhabits mountain streams and moist forests; designated a Japanese National Natural Monument. Endangered from habitat loss and collection for the international pet trade.

children.name: Geoemyda spengleri
children.description: A tiny turtle, rarely exceeding 12 cm, with a flattened serrated carapace coloured brown or olive and a jet-black plastron. Inhabits evergreen and bamboo forests of southern China, Vietnam, Laos, and northeastern Thailand, near trickling streams. Endangered from massive overcollection for traditional medicine and the pet trade.

children.name: Hardella
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Brahminy river turtle of the Indian subcontinent. It is a large, herbivorous freshwater turtle inhabiting the Ganges and Brahmaputra river systems. The genus was once considered part of Kachuga or Batagur, but molecular data support its status as a distinct lineage.

children.name: Hardella thurjii
children.description: A large river turtle reaching 50 cm, with a dark brown carapace, a yellow plastron, and a distinctive yellow stripe behind the eye. Found in the Indus, Ganges, and Brahmaputra basins of Pakistan, India, Bangladesh, and Nepal. Inhabits deep rivers and large canals; vulnerable from fishing bycatch, habitat degradation, and egg collection.

children.name: Heosemys
children.description: Forest and pond turtles of Southeast Asia — a diverse genus ranging from the massive Giant Asian Pond Turtle to the spiny turtle with its sharply pointed marginal scutes. They inhabit lowland and hill forests, swamps, and slow-moving waterways. All four species are threatened by habitat loss and overcollection for food and the pet trade.

children.name: Heosemys annandalii
children.description: A very large freshwater turtle reaching 50 cm, with a broad brown carapace and a yellow head with a distinctive pale patch behind each eye. Found in Thailand, Cambodia, Laos, and Vietnam, inhabiting lowland swamps and slow-moving rivers. Critically endangered from habitat loss, pollution, and overcollection for food.

children.name: Heosemys depressa
children.description: A medium-sized turtle with a distinctly flattened, depressed carapace — unique among Heosemys — coloured dark brown or olive. Endemic to the Arakan Hills of western Myanmar, where it inhabits evergreen forests and hill streams. Critically endangered; once thought extinct until rediscovered in the 1990s, then heavily targeted by collectors.

children.name: Heosemys grandis
children.description: One of the largest geoemydids, reaching 45 cm and weighing up to 10 kg, with a greyish-brown carapace and a light-coloured head. Ranges from Myanmar and Thailand through Cambodia, Laos, and Vietnam to Malaysia. Inhabits lowland rivers, swamps, and marshes; vulnerable from overharvesting for food and habitat destruction.

children.name: Heosemys spinosa
children.description: Instantly recognisable by the sharp, spine-like projections along the margin of the carapace, which are especially prominent in juveniles and become blunter with age. Found in Myanmar, Thailand, Malaysia, Singapore, Indonesia, Brunei, and the Philippines. Inhabits hill streams in evergreen forests; endangered from habitat loss and pet trade collection.

children.name: Leucocephalon
children.description: A monotypic genus endemic to Sulawesi, Indonesia, containing the Sulawesi forest turtle. It was formerly assigned to Geoemyda or Heosemys but is now recognised as a distinct lineage. The genus name means 'white head', a reference to the pale coloration of the head in adults.

children.name: Leucocephalon yuwonoi
children.description: A striking turtle with a dark brown to black carapace and a white or pale grey head — the source of both its common and scientific names. Endemic to Sulawesi, Indonesia, inhabiting mountain streams in tropical forests at elevations of 400–1000 m. Critically endangered from deforestation and collection for the pet trade.

children.name: Malayemys
children.description: Snail-eating turtles of Southeast Asia — small to medium-sized freshwater turtles specialised for feeding on molluscs, with powerful jaws adapted to crushing snail shells. They inhabit lowland wetlands, rice paddies, and slow-moving waterways. Once considered monotypic, recent revisions recognise three distinct species.

children.name: Malayemys khoratensis
children.description: A recently described species distinguished from its congeners by subtle shell and head pattern differences. Found on the Khorat Plateau of northeastern Thailand and adjacent Laos. Inhabits rice paddies, marshes, and slow-moving canals; threatened by wetland conversion and collection for food.

children.name: Malayemys macrocephala
children.description: A small turtle with a large head relative to body size, a brown carapace, and bold yellow head stripes including a broad stripe under the eye. Found in Myanmar, Thailand, Malaysia, Cambodia, and Vietnam. Inhabits rice paddies, irrigation canals, and marshes; threatened by agricultural intensification and harvesting for food.

children.name: Malayemys subtrijuga
children.description: Similar to M. macrocephala but with a narrower head and different head stripe pattern. Occurs in the Mekong River basin of Thailand, Laos, Cambodia, and Vietnam. Inhabits wetlands, rice paddies, and slow rivers; threatened by habitat loss and overharvesting for local consumption and the pet trade.

children.name: Mauremys
children.description: Striped-neck pond turtles of the Mediterranean, Middle East, and East Asia — the most species-rich turtle genus in the Palearctic. Mauremys species are characterised by the yellow or olive stripes running along the neck and are highly adapted to warm, slow-moving or standing fresh water. Several species are critically threatened by habitat loss and hybridisation with introduced relatives.

children.name: Mauremys nigricans
children.description: A medium-sized turtle with a dark brown to black carapace and a reddish or orange head and neck with light yellow stripes. Endemic to southern China, inhabiting ponds and slow-moving rivers. Endangered from overcollection for traditional medicine and the pet trade; heavily farmed in captivity.

children.name: Melanochelys
children.description: Hill and black turtles of South Asia — medium-sized turtles that inhabit forest streams and hill slopes of the Indian subcontinent. The two species differ markedly in appearance: one is uniformly black, the other bears three distinct keels. Both are threatened by habitat loss and collection for food and the pet trade.

children.name: Melanochelys tricarinata
children.description: Named for its three prominent keels running the length of the carapace, giving a ridged appearance. The carapace is brown with a yellow rim. Found in northeastern India, Bangladesh, and Nepal, inhabiting hill forests and streams. Endangered from deforestation and collection for the pet trade.

children.name: Melanochelys trijuga
children.description: A medium-sized turtle with a dark brown to black carapace and a black head with cream or yellow markings. Widely distributed across India, Sri Lanka, Bangladesh, Nepal, and Myanmar. Inhabits a variety of freshwater habitats including ponds, marshes, and rice paddies; relatively adaptable but declining in parts of its range.

children.name: Morenia
children.description: Eyed turtles of South and Southeast Asia — small freshwater turtles named for the prominent ocelli (eyespots) on the carapace of one species. They are primarily herbivorous and inhabit slow-moving lowland waters. Both species are threatened by wetland loss and harvesting.

children.name: Morenia ocellata
children.description: A small turtle with a distinctive pattern of ocelli — eye-like rings — on the carapace, which are yellowish with dark centres on a brown background. Endemic to Myanmar, inhabiting lowland rivers, ponds, and canals. Endangered from habitat loss and overcollection for food and the pet trade.

children.name: Morenia petersi
children.description: A small turtle with a brown carapace bearing faint ocelli that fade with age, and a yellowish head with dark markings. Found in the Ganges and Brahmaputra basins of India, Bangladesh, and Nepal. Inhabits slow-moving rivers, oxbow lakes, and ponds; threatened by wetland degradation and bycatch.

children.name: Notochelys
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Malayan flat-shelled turtle, distinguished by its remarkably flattened carapace and a hinge on the plastron. It is a secretive, semi-aquatic turtle of Southeast Asian lowland forests, spending much of its time buried in leaf litter or mud.

children.name: Notochelys platynota
children.description: Easily identified by its extremely flattened, oval carapace which is olive to brown with dark blotches, and a hinged plastron. Found in Myanmar, Thailand, Malaysia, Singapore, Indonesia, Brunei, and Vietnam. Inhabits lowland swamps, peat bogs, and slow-moving streams; vulnerable from habitat loss and collection for food.

children.name: Orlitia
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Malaysian giant turtle, one of the largest freshwater turtles in Asia. It is a powerful swimmer inhabiting large rivers and lakes in Southeast Asia. The species has been heavily exploited for food, leading to drastic population declines across its range.

children.name: Orlitia borneensis
children.description: A massive turtle reaching 80 cm carapace length and 50 kg, with a dark brown to black carapace and a pale yellow to white plastron. Found in Malaysia, Indonesia (Sumatra, Borneo), and possibly Thailand. Inhabits large lowland rivers, lakes, and swamps; critically endangered from overhunting for the Asian food trade.

children.name: Pangshura
children.description: Roofed turtles of South Asia — small to medium-sized turtles with a distinctively elevated, roof-like carapace that gives the genus its common name. They inhabit rivers and streams of the Indian subcontinent, nesting on sandy banks. Formerly included in the genus Kachuga, they are now recognised as a distinct lineage.

children.name: Pangshura smithii
children.description: A small roofed turtle with an olive-brown carapace bearing a low dorsal keel, and a pale yellow head with black-edged markings. Found in Pakistan, India, Bangladesh, and Nepal. Inhabits rivers and streams; one of the few South Asian turtles with ZZ/ZW sex determination.

children.name: Pangshura sylhetensis
children.description: A small turtle with a strongly keeled, elevated carapace and a pale yellow head with a red patch behind the eye. Endemic to northeastern India and Bangladesh, restricted to a small area in Assam and Sylhet. Endangered from habitat loss and collection; one of the least studied Asian turtles.

children.name: Pangshura tecta
children.description: A medium-sized roofed turtle with a prominently arched carapace bearing a strong central keel, olive to brown in colour. Found in the Ganges, Brahmaputra, and Indus basins of India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Nepal. Inhabits rivers, ponds, and canals; relatively common but declining from habitat degradation.

children.name: Pangshura tentoria
children.description: A small roofed turtle with a tent-like carapace shape and a distinctive vertebral keel, coloured olive to brown with darker blotches. Widely distributed in the river systems of central and northern India, Nepal, and Bangladesh. Inhabits slow-flowing rivers with sandy bottoms; threatened by sand mining and water extraction.

children.name: Rhinoclemmys
children.description: Neotropical wood turtles — the only geoemydid genus in the Americas, ranging from Mexico through Central America to northern South America. They are semi-terrestrial to terrestrial turtles inhabiting tropical forests, where many species have beautifully patterned shells that provide camouflage in the leaf litter. The genus comprises nine extant species adapted to diverse habitats from dry scrub to rainforest.

children.name: Rhinoclemmys annulata
children.description: A medium-sized turtle with a brown carapace bearing faint lighter rings on each scute. Found from Honduras through Central America to Ecuador. Terrestrial, inhabiting lowland and montane rainforests up to 1500 m.

children.name: Rhinoclemmys areolata
children.description: A small wood turtle with a brown carapace marked with deep furrows and yellow areolae on each scute. Found in the Yucatán Peninsula of Mexico, Belize, Guatemala, and northern Honduras. Inhabits dry forests and scrub; terrestrial and secretive.

children.name: Rhinoclemmys diademata
children.description: A medium-sized turtle with a brown carapace and a distinctive crown-like pattern of yellow stripes on the head. Endemic to the Lake Maracaibo basin of Venezuela and Colombia. Inhabits swamps, marshes, and slow-moving streams.

children.name: Rhinoclemmys funerea
children.description: A large Rhinoclemmys with a very dark brown to black carapace and a black head with faint yellow markings. Found in the Caribbean drainages of Nicaragua, Costa Rica, and Panama. Semi-aquatic, inhabiting rivers and swamps in humid lowland forests.

children.name: Rhinoclemmys melanosterna
children.description: A medium-sized turtle with a brown carapace and a dark head with yellow or red markings. Ranges from Panama through Colombia to Ecuador. Inhabits rivers and streams in lowland and mid-elevation forests.

children.name: Rhinoclemmys nasuta
children.description: Named for its prominent, projecting snout, which is longer than in other Rhinoclemmys species. Has a brown carapace and a uniformly coloured head. Endemic to the Chocó region of western Colombia and Ecuador. Inhabits rainforest streams.

children.name: Rhinoclemmys pulcherrima
children.description: The most colourful Rhinoclemmys, with a brown carapace bearing bright red, orange, and yellow markings on the marginal scutes, and a boldly patterned head. Found from Mexico to Costa Rica. Semi-terrestrial in dry and moist forests; popular in the pet trade.

children.name: Rhinoclemmys punctularia
children.description: A medium-sized turtle with a brown carapace, a blackish head with yellow spots, and yellow spots on the forelimbs. Found in Venezuela, Guyana, Suriname, French Guiana, and northern Brazil. Inhabits lowland rainforest streams and swamps.

children.name: Rhinoclemmys rubida
children.description: A small wood turtle with a brown carapace bearing reddish-orange markings and a head with bright red or orange spots. Endemic to western Mexico in the states of Chiapas, Oaxaca, Michoacán, and Jalisco. Inhabits dry forests and oak woodlands near streams.

children.name: Sacalia
children.description: Eyed turtles of southern China and Southeast Asia — small, secretive freshwater turtles named for the striking ocellus-like markings on their heads. They are among the least studied geoemydids, inhabiting clear, fast-moving mountain streams where they are rarely observed. All species are endangered, primarily through collection for the Chinese medicine trade.

children.name: Siebenrockiella
children.description: Marsh and forest turtles of Southeast Asia — two very different species united by molecular phylogenetics. The black marsh turtle is a widespread, aquatic species with a distinctive smile-like mouth line, while the Philippine forest turtle is endemic to Palawan. Both are threatened by habitat loss and overcollection for the pet trade.

children.name: Siebenrockiella crassicollis
children.description: A medium-sized, highly aquatic turtle with a jet-black carapace and a distinctive white or cream-coloured 'smile' marking on the head. Found in Myanmar, Thailand, Cambodia, Laos, Vietnam, Malaysia, Singapore, Indonesia, and the Philippines. Inhabits swamps, marshes, and slow-moving waterways; exhibits XX/XY sex determination.

children.name: Siebenrockiella leytensis
children.description: A strikingly patterned turtle with a brown carapace bearing bold yellow markings and a yellow head with dark blotches. Endemic to the island of Palawan in the Philippines, inhabiting forest streams and creeks. Critically endangered; heavily targeted by collectors, with an estimated 90% population decline.

children.name: Vijayachelys
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the cane turtle of the Western Ghats, India — a small, secretive forest turtle named for its habit of hiding among sugarcane plantations. It was formerly placed in Geoemyda or Heosemys before being assigned to its own genus. It is one of the most evolutionarily distinct turtles in Asia.

children.name: Vijayachelys silvatica
children.description: A very small turtle, reaching only 13 cm, with a domed brown carapace, a yellow plastron, and a yellow-brown head. Endemic to the Western Ghats of Kerala and Tamil Nadu, India, inhabiting evergreen forests and plantations. Endangered from habitat loss and collection for the pet trade; one of the least-studied Indian turtles.
